messages.subject: [R-sig-genetics] R-sig-genetics Digest, Vol 7, Issue 1
messages.body_plain:
No, but I have a tool in Galaxy based on Abecasis' GRR - his software
still available but only as 32bit windows app so very limited in terms
of sample and genotype sizes.
My app reimplements his algorithm - It estimates mean/sd allele
sharing for all pairs, then normalizes those for each pedigree
relationship group (parent-child/sibs/unrelated/etc..) and does a nice
interactive plot.
You'll need at least 5k or 10k ld independent SNPs for reasonable results.
It's in the rgenetics tools at http://usegalaxy.org - happy to help if
you have any questions on using it.

On Mon, Jun 28, 2010 at 8:00 PM,  <r-sig-genetics-request at r-project.org> wrote:

messages.subject: [R-sig-genetics] R-sig-genetics Digest, Vol 7, Issue 1
messages.body_plain:
Thanks for the offer Ross,

I'm looking at 8-18 locus microsat data.  I can undertake the 
calculations easily enough elsewhere,.  However if there was a function 
out there already I would have used it since most of my data 
manipulation and calculations are being handled in R: and it would have 
made things neater.

messages.body_snippet:
Thanks for the offer Ross,

I'm looking at 8-18 locus microsat data.  I can undertake the 
calculations easily enough elsewhere,.  However if there was a function 
out there already I would have used

messages.subject: [R-sig-genetics] Re : Calcualtion of pairwise relatedness in R: > package?
messages.subject_clean: Re : Calcualtion of pairwise relatedness in R: > package?
messages.body_plain:
On Mon, Jun 28, 2010 at 12:04 PM, Nevil Amos <nevil.amos at gmail.com> wrote:
> Thanks for the offer Ross,
>
> I'm looking at 8-18 locus microsat data. ?I can undertake the calculations
> easily enough elsewhere,. ?However if there was a function out there already
> I would have used it since most of my data manipulation and calculations are
> being handled in R: and it would have made things neater.

You could format and prepare your data within R, output to a text file
and then make a system call to your external program(s) from within R
to perform the calculations before reading them back into R for
summarising and presenting.

A bit of work I'd imagine, but I'd guess it has the potential of being
developed into a package for subsequent use by yourself and others.

Also whilst typing that I remembered that Shaun Purcell's Plink (see
http://pngu.mgh.harvard.edu/~purcell/plink/) has some integration with
R, although its mainly geared towards analysis of SNPs and CNVs (but
given that it can handle multi-marker haplotypes it should be capable
of handling multi-allelic microsatellites).

Regards

Neil
-- 
"... no scientific worker has a fixed level of significance at which
from year to year, and in all circumstances, he rejects hypotheses; he
rather gives his mind to each particular case in the light of his
evidence and his ideas." - Sir Ronald A. Fisher (1956)

Email - nshephard at gmail.com
Website - http://slack.ser.man.ac.uk/
Photos - http://www.flickr.com/photos/slackline/

messages.subject: [R-sig-genetics] R-sig-genetics Digest, Vol 7, Issue 1
messages.body_plain:
ah - the rgGRR Galaxy code uses python/weave C code and is limited to
biallelics so easy to estimate alleles shared as 0,1 or 2 -
interesting to see how that will work for microsatellites. Probably
not too hard to write R code to do the calculations but I don't know
of any existing routines..
